title: Prayer, Part 6: How Should We Pray, and What Rule Has God Given?
speaker: Mr. Alex Lyro

outline.point: I. WLC Q185: How Are We to Pray? (Fifteen Characteristics)
subpoints: A. With an awful apprehension of God's majesty — reverential fear, not casual familiarity
B. With a deep sense of our own unworthiness, necessities, and sins
C. With penitent hearts — not merely confessing sin but hating and turning from it

outline.point: II. With Thankful, Enlarged, and Understanding Hearts
subpoints: A. Thankful hearts: specific and genuine recognition of God's gifts, not just a formula
B. Enlarged hearts: a deep desire to receive God's blessings, like Hannah pouring out her soul
C. Understanding: praying with the mind, knowing what we ask — ignorant prayer is not pious

outline.point: III. With Faith, Sincerity, Fervency, and Love
subpoints: A. Faith is arguably the most essential: to pray without faith is not to pray at all (Hebrews 11:6)
B. Sincerity: no performance, no pretense — bring the real heart, not the polished one
C. Fervency and love: earnestness without theatrical display; love guards against empty duty

outline.point: IV. With Perseverance and Humble Submission to God's Will
subpoints: A. Perseverance: keep praying even when nothing happens; waiting is active trust, not passivity
B. Waiting upon God protects against both despair and anxiety (Psalm 27:14; 1 Peter 5:7)
C. Humble submission: not my will but yours — embrace God's no, not yet, and wait

outline.point: V. WLC Q186: What Rule Has God Given for Prayer?
subpoints: A. The whole Word of God directs prayer: its commands, promises, examples, and boundaries
B. The special rule of direction is the Lord's Prayer — the only prayer Jesus explicitly taught
C. The Lord's Prayer is a pattern, not a magic formula; its purpose is to shape theology-driven prayer

summary:
In Part 6 of the prayer series, Mr. Alex Lyro addresses Westminster Larger Catechism questions 185 and 186, which describe how Christians are to pray and what rule God has given for prayer. He works through fifteen characteristics of prayer from Q185, emphasizing that all of them begin before prayer itself — they reflect a whole-life orientation toward God that shapes the soul long before we kneel. Among the most important: reverential awe of God's majesty, honest consciousness of our own unworthiness, penitent and thankful hearts, and the faith that anchors prayer in the character and promises of God rather than in our own feelings or performance.

For Q186, Lyro explains that the entire Word of God directs prayer — it commands prayer, promises answers, records examples, and sets boundaries. But the special rule is the Lord's Prayer, the only prayer Christ explicitly taught His disciples. Its purpose is not mindless repetition but theological shaping: to keep our prayers from being self-centered, aimless, or shallow, and to ground them in God's glory. He closes with five application points encouraging the congregation to grow in prayer as a whole-life discipline.
transcript:
## Opening Prayer

Heavenly Father, I thank you that we can be here. I thank you that you have given us one day in seven to come before you, that you have lowered yourself to let us be able to commune with you. I pray that you would make this lesson be edifying to your people, but most of all would glorify you. May you keep me from getting in the way of your truth, and that you would prepare us to worship you truly later this morning. In Jesus' name, Amen.

## Introduction

So a couple of things to begin with. Number one, and I'm pretty sure this has been said by everyone in this series so far: I'm not a master on prayer. I'm barely even a novice. Prayer is arguably, if not the most difficult spiritual discipline in the Christian life. I am not an expert on prayer. This lesson is, I guarantee you, more convicting for me than any of you.

So this morning we are diving back into our study on the Westminster Larger Catechism dealing with questions 185 and 186. They cover what kind of heart a Christian should pray with, and what rule guides us in prayer. We're going to walk phrase by phrase through these: how are we to pray, and what rule has God given us?

## WLC Q185: How Are We to Pray?

Question 185 asks, how are we to pray? We are to pray with an awful apprehension of the majesty of God, and deep sense of our own unworthiness, necessities, and sins. With penitent, thankful, and enlarged hearts. With understanding, faith, sincerity, fervency, love, and perseverance. Waiting upon him with humble submission to his will.

I have fifteen points here — just breaking this down phrase by phrase.

### 1. Awful Apprehension of the Majesty of God

As we know here, "awful" does not mean terrible or horrible — it means full of awe, having reverential fear. Hebrews 12:28-29: let us have grace by which we may serve God acceptably with reverence and godly fear. For our God is a consuming fire. Calvin says in his Institutes, men will never worship God with sincerity until they properly understand his majesty. In America today, the only thing that's focused on is God's love. But God is majestic, powerful, infinite, eternal, great. He is loving, but that is just one aspect of God. Loss says the first rule of true prayer is reverence and awareness that we are addressing the infinite personal God. Ecclesiastes 5:1-2: do not be rash with your mouth and let not your heart utter anything hastily before God. For God is in heaven and you on earth, therefore let your words be few.

### 2. Deep Sense of Our Own Unworthiness

This is not self-hatred or self-pity — this is humility. Abraham, when he was talking to God about Sodom and Gomorrah, recognized his own place: then Abraham answered and said, indeed now, I who am but dust and ashes have taken it upon myself to speak to the Lord (Genesis 18:27). John Owen writes: he who would pray rightly must bring a broken will and a sense of creature poverty. When you realize who God is, naturally you realize who you are not.

### 3. Deep Sense of Our Necessities and Sins

This is an honest consciousness of our condition — not only physically but spiritually. The tax collector in Luke 18 had a deep sense that his greatest necessity was for the mercy of God, and that affected his prayer. We cannot pray rightly without confessing sin. Thomas Watson says: the plaster of Christ's blood is never applied until the wound of sin is open. If you're not willing to admit you have a wound, you will not accept medical care.

### 4-5. Penitent and Thankful Hearts

Repentance is not merely confessing sin but hating it and turning from it. Joel 2:12-13: turn to me with all your heart, with fasting, with weeping and with mourning. So rend your heart and not your garments. And alongside repentance, thanksgiving should saturate our prayer. 1 Thessalonians 5:16-18: rejoice always, pray without ceasing, in everything give thanks. Colossians 4:2: continue earnestly in prayer, being vigilant in it with thanksgiving. Philippians 4:6: in everything by prayer and supplication, with thanksgiving, let your requests be made known to God. Thankfulness is more than just "Lord, thank you for this day." Be specific.

### 6. Enlarged Heart

This is a heart with a deep desire to receive God's blessings and experience communion with him. 1 Samuel 1:15: Hannah answered and said, no my Lord, I am a woman of sorrowful spirit. I have drunk neither wine nor intoxicating drink, but have poured out my soul before the Lord. That is an enlarged heart. She is praying with her whole heart, pouring her soul out in faith that he will answer.

### 7. Understanding

We pray with our minds. Paul says in 1 Corinthians 14:15: I will pray with the Spirit and I will also pray with understanding. Voss says, ignorant prayer is not pious. It is careless. Know what you are asking for; pray Scripture and pray thoughtfully.

### 8. Faith

Faith is essential — arguably the most important attribute of Christian prayer. To pray without faith is not to pray at all. Hebrews 11:6: without faith it is impossible to please him, for he who comes to God must believe that he is and that he is a rewarder of those who diligently seek him. Calvin says: prayer flows from faith, and faith is strengthened by prayer.

### 9. Sincerity

This means no pretense, no performance. Matthew 6 lays this out clearly: don't practice righteousness to be seen by others. Don't pray publicly in order to showcase holiness. John Owen warns: God is not moved by the noise of the words, but by the sincerity of the heart. Bring your real heart to God, not the polished one.

### 10. Fervency

This means earnestness and warmth — not emotional display, intensity of volume, or dramatic expression. It is a matter of the heart. Without fervency, prayer is motion. With it, prayer is worship.

### 11. Love

Praying with love guards against empty duty. Matthew 15:8 — these people draw near to me with their mouth and honor me with their lips, but their heart is far from me. We pray not because we have to, but because we get to. And love for others leads you to pray for more than just yourself. James 5:16: confess your trespasses to one another and pray for one another.

### 12-13. Perseverance and Waiting upon God

Keep praying even when nothing happens. Luke 18:1: he spoke a parable to them that men always ought to pray and not lose heart. Waiting is active trust, not passivity. Psalm 27:14: wait on the Lord. Be of good courage and he shall strengthen your heart. Isaiah 40:31: those who wait on the Lord shall renew their strength. They shall mount up with wings like eagles.

Waiting upon the Lord protects us from despair: when we wait humbly, our hope is not in the outcome but in the Lord. And it protects us from anxiety: Psalm 94:19, in the multitude of my anxieties within me, your comforts delight my soul. 1 Peter 5:7: cast all your cares on him because he cares for you. You have to let go when you hand your cares to God.

### 14-15. Humble Submission to God's Will

Our prayers end where Jesus ended: not my will but yours be done (Matthew 26:39). 2 Corinthians 12:8-10: Paul asked three times for the thorn in the flesh to depart. And he said, my grace is sufficient for you. Hold your requests with open hands. God's way is always better.

## WLC Q186: What Rule Has God Given for Prayer?

Question 186 asks, what rule has God given us for our direction in the duty of prayer? The answer is: the whole Word of God is of use to direct us in the duty of prayer, but the special rule of direction is that form of prayer which our Savior Christ taught his disciples, commonly called the Lord's Prayer.

The whole Bible shapes everything about prayer — not just that we pray, but why, how, and for what. Scripture commands us to pray. It records examples that model prayer. It sets boundaries that keep prayer God-centered. We are not inventing prayer — we are responding to God's revelation.

But the special rule of direction is the Lord's Prayer. Why? It is the only prayer Jesus explicitly taught his disciples. It summarizes every category of biblical prayer. It guards against self-centered, aimless, or shallow prayer.

This does not mean the Lord's Prayer is the only prayer we may pray. It does mean that the Lord's Prayer is a pattern and a measure of praying. Jesus did not only teach that we should pray, but how we are to pray — not as performance, and not in empty repetition. His goal was to shape our prayers by theology, by our knowledge of the Bible and our knowledge of God.

## Five Parting Shots

All of this begins before prayer. You live your life like this, your prayer grows. When your prayer grows, your life grows.

Five ways to grow in prayer: Begin with God's majesty. Be honest about your sin and your needs. Pray with mind and heart. Use Scripture, especially the Lord's Prayer, to shape your petitions. Rest humbly in God's will.

Let us close with a prayer from the Valley of Vision, a collection of Puritan prayers: O God of the open ear, teach me to live by prayer as well as by Providence, for myself, soul, body, children, family, church. Give me a heart frameable to thy will, so that I may live in prayer and honor thee, being kept from evil known and unknown. Help me to see the sin that accompanies all I do, and the good I can distill from everything. Work in prayer to bring my will to thine, and that without this it is folly to pray. Teach me that it is wisdom for me to pray for all I have out of love, willingly, not of necessity, that I may come to thee at any time to lay open my needs. Amen.
